```
from __future__ import print_function, division
import imgaug as ia
from imgaug import augmenters as iaa
from scipy import misc, ndimage
import numpy as np

s = 150  # size of the image
pd = 25  # positions of each corner pixel (e.g. at (25, 25), (-25, 25), ...)
ps = 4  # size of each square to draw
img = np.zeros((s, s, 3), dtype=np.uint8)  # black example image
# add white squares in the corners
img[pd - ps:pd, pd - ps:pd, :] = 255
img[-pd - ps:-pd, pd - ps:pd, :] = 255
img[pd - ps:pd, -pd - ps:-pd, :] = 255
img[-pd - ps:-pd, -pd - ps:-pd, :] = 255

# define one test keypoint at (100, 100)
kp_x = 100
kp_y = 100
kps = [ia.KeypointsOnImage([ia.Keypoint(kp_x, kp_y)], shape=img.shape)]
img_with_kp = np.copy(img)
img_with_kp[kp_y - ps:kp_y, kp_x - ps:kp_x, 1] = 255

nb_augs = 1000  # how often to augment here
aug_seq = iaa.Sequential({
    iaa.Affine(rotate=(-40, 40))  # rotate -40 to 40 deg
})
aug_aff = iaa.Affine(rotate=(-40, 40))

imgs_seq_aug = aug_seq.augment_image(img)
imgs_aff_aug = aug_aff.augment_image(img)

# augment the keypoints N times
kps_seq_aug = []
kps_aff_aug = []
kps_seq_aug = aug_seq.augment_keypoints(kps)
kps_aff_aug = aug_aff.augment_keypoints(kps)
seq_x, seq_y = kps_seq_aug[0].keypoints[0].x, kps_seq_aug[0].keypoints[0].y
aff_x, aff_y = kps_aff_aug[0].keypoints[0].x, kps_aff_aug[0].keypoints[0].y

# code to test draw_on_image
img_org = kps[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(img_with_kp))
img_seq_aug = kps_seq_aug[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(imgs_seq_aug))
img_aff_aug = kps_aff_aug[0].draw_on_image(np.copy(imgs_aff_aug))
# misc.imshow(np.hstack([img_seq_aug, img_aff_aug, img_org.astype(np.uint8)]))

# plot
misc.imsave("test_image.png", np.hstack([img_seq_aug, img_aff_aug, img_org]))

```

I don't mind your second implementation at all. My original implementation allowed for more than one function to be registered under the same hook in the same module, but I think don't think that's actually a relevant feature.

If I'm reading [your implementation right](https://gist.github.com/berdario/f91fb8f212420cd00f75#file-_utils-py-L19), you search `$VIRTUAL_ENV` and `$VIRTUAL_ENV/inner` for a python module named `hooks`. I kind of liked what my implementation did here, it allowed for a simple `$VIRTUAL_ENV/hooks.py` or executed every module under `$VIRTUAL_ENV/hooks/`. This allows for hook files to be distributed independently, and installation is as simple as dropping the file into `$VIRTUAL_ENV/hooks`.

I'm also of the opinion that the `priority` functionality is a bit overkill at this stage. Hooks should be more or less independent in terms of execution order, and it's fairly trivial to execute things in a specific order if an environment really demands it.

Finally, what are your thoughts on searching `$PROJECT_DIR/.pew/` and then falling back to `$VIRTUAL_ENV/pew/`? This means I can check in hooks directly into my repo and anyone that clones it and uses pew automatically benefits from having the hooks necessary for my environment.

This is not a real error. The property in question is populated at runtime by the base packet class based on the value of __hdr__.

> I doubt you'll ever encounter a TLS Handshake packet in the wild that
has no extensions. It is not permitted by protocol, and it would be
impossible to establish a TLS connection without the use of extensions
in the Handshake packet.

The idea is that dpkt must be resilient to malformed packets. 

> Section 7.4.1.4 of RFC 5246 says:
When multiple extensions of different types are present in the
ClientHello or ServerHello messages, the extensions MAY appear in any
order.  There MUST NOT be more than one extension of the same type.

Again, we can't just blindly trust RFC when parsing packets. They can be malformed. Pcap files can be cut in the middle etc. 

> Given the above I think using a Python dict makes sense.

Just trying to keep it consistent with other parsers (I think DHCP options is an example). It's trivial to convert an ordered list of tuples to a dict, just be calling dict() on it, if someone needs it in their app implementation. However it's impossible to convert it in reverse while maintaining order and keeping duplicates intact.

Maybe it is possible to create a more modular approach for the EDA tool script. The value of FuseSoc for me is to collect all the RTL and other files needed for a project and add it to a EDA tool like Vivado. 
So all the "read_vhdl" and "read_xdc" lines are the interesting ones. The Workflow steps "around" like Project creation, synthesis innvocation are the ones which I need to adapt often.

A better approach would be to define a  user defined "toplevel script" which is executed on e.g. the build command, and leave it to the responsibility of this script to invoke the  generated the file setup script 

E.g. a Viado section could look like
````
[vivado]
project_script=myscript.tcl
addfiles_script_name=addfiles

````
myscript.tcl would then setup the project would source the auto generated addfiles.tcl
When project_script is not specified fusesoc will automatically create a generic one, which is similar to todays script

Maybe the idea on this form is also "hacky" and very much focused to my individual requirements. But maybe you get the concept. Another option would be some "templating mechanism" to provide user templates for the script.
I think the "hard coded" project scripts as current will always suffer from being very static and will lead to a lot of hard to maintain feature requests over time.
Remember our discussion about ISE bmm files. And there are lot of other cases. 
For example in my tcl script I added 
````
set_property board_part digilentinc.com:arty:part0:1.1 [current_project]
set_property target_language VHDL `[current_project]``
set_property default_lib work [current_project]
set_property  ip_repo_paths  $script_folder/../ip_repo [current_project]
````
which is not possbile to setup in the vivado section currently. But others may  require completly different settings to get their projects running

thread but I agree completely with your reasoning on the decision to merge.
One point of note with respect to the unit tests is that I would definitely
expect intermittent failures when using an external DBus daemon with a
non-trial based unit testing tool. The only real enhancement made by trial
is the ability to return a Deferred to the result of the test rather than
having it complete immediately. Given the way the code is structured, I can
believe that non-async based unit testing frameworks would generally either
"get lucky" where the tests would complete before the function returns or
it would provide false successes in that the method would complete almost
immediately and therefore show success but not actually run some or all of
the test code. So using anything other than trial is iffy at best unless
the testing framework explicitly includes support for a trial plugin.
pytest has that, I believe, but I'm not sure about others. Personally, I've
always just used trial.

Tom

@clatcho I would probably just use the `send_command_timing` method which basically will default to waiting a certain amount of time and then give up.

You can use `delay_factor` and `max_loops` to influence how long it waits. In general, the pattern is it waits a certain amount of time for the first data and then some amount of time after that.

```
net_connect.send_command_timing("traceroute 8.8.8.8")
```

If that doesn't work, I would probably try:

```
try:
    net_connect.send_command("traceroute 8.8.8.8")
except IOError:
    # catch error
    pass
```

And then use `max_loops` to influence how long I will wait. I think it will default to waiting about 90 seconds.

> How tinydb from python updates the data everytime we use .update() ? it updates an specific sector or the whole file all the time? isn't this risky?

Yes, the JSONStorage of TinyDB always reads and writes the whole file. As @eugene-eeo mentioned, you could first write to a temporary file and then rename it to the file TinyDB uses for atomicity.

I defenitely think it's useful but I'm not sure how to implement this best. I wouldn't make this a default, though, because that would break usages where a conversion is not expected. Instead one could make use of TinyDB's extensibility. At the moment I can think of these options:

- Create a custom storage (copying the source of `JSONStorage`) that uses a custom JSON encoder/decoder
- Implement the encoder/decoder as a middleware. It would scan the data for `datetime` objects when writing and for date-like strings when reading and convert between these two

```python
async def main():
    out = Channel()
    stop = asyncio.Event()
    asyncio.ensure_future(producer(out, stop))
    for _ in range(3):
        print("from out: {}".format(await out.get()))
    stop.set()

async def producer(out, stop):
    i = 0
    while not stop.is_set():
        done, pending = await asyncio.wait([stop.wait(), out.put(i)], return_when=asyncio.FIRST_COMPLETED)
        [p.cancel() for p in pending]
        if stop.is_set():
            out.close()
            break
        else:
            i += 1

loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
loop.run_until_complete(main())
loop.close()
```

I don't undertand why you'd need the channel returned to you :confused:

It seems like you can solve the problem just as easily and readable using the standard library.

The reason I'm uncomfortable with this change, is that I only see two solutions for implementing `select` in the currently discussed form:
- `Channel` needs a breaking change where `put()` and `get()` also returns their own channel
- `Future` returned by `get()` and `put()` needs to hack a reference to the channel onto the `Future`

I don't want to break backwards compat, and I _really_ don't want to hack `Future`.

If you can come up with a way that doesn't break backwards compat or hacks values onto stdlib objects, I'd be a lot more open to adding some kind of `select` functionality.

Hello!

I thought about this some more. I think it makes sense for the middleware to entirely handle CORS preflight requests itself, without passing them on to the next component. Because if it had to pass on the preflight requests to some other component, so that the other component could help handle them, then the CORS middleware wouldn't be doing its job. Handling these requests is its raison d'etre, after all. So I'll send a PR to change it so that it only handles CORS preflight OPTIONS requests itself, not all OPTIONS requests. It'll be pretty simple: just check for the Access-Control-Request-Foo headers.

Regarding whether an OPTIONS request can be CORS preflight and some other sort of OPTIONS request at the same time - I don't understand the CORS specification properly on this point, but I think the two can't be combined. The preflight request is asking whether a script has permission to perform the real request - it's not actually doing a real OPTIONS request. So I think these are two conceptually separate things. However, I'd really appreciate the opinion of someone who understood the CORS specification properly, which I'm sorry I don't :-)

> God, are you forced to used OFXv102?

As far as I can tell, yes I am. Fun times.

>Have you got a reference backing up your assertion?

The two things that caught my eye in the spec is section 1.2.2 ("Request and Response Model") and section 2.5.1.1 ("Signon Request <SONRQ>") from Open Financial Exchange Specification 1.0.2. I've uploaded the section 2.5.1.1 to make it easier for your reference but you can also get it at http://www.ofx.net/downloads.html:

![2 5 1 1](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1291387/41493918-7f487176-7104-11e8-8d1b-50d59816b62d.png)

If you note, DTCLIENT, USERID, USERPASS, LANGUAGE, APPID, and APPVER all do _not_ have closing tags noted, whereas SONRQ and FI do. This matches the behavior seen.

Section 1.2.2 also says,
> The key rule of Open Financial Exchange syntax is that each tag is either an element or an aggregate. Data follows its element tag. An aggregate tag begins a compound tag sequence, which must end with a matching tag; for example, <AGGREGATE> ... </AGGREGATE>. 

The term "aggregate" matches the description used in section 2.5.1.1 and requires the ending tag. I will also admit that I've not been able to find a place in the spec that explicitly says that element tags must _not_ end with a matching tag.

It's entirely possible that BECU hasn't correctly implemented the spec, however I started by using mitmproxy with Banktivity to see how they were (successfully) connecting and they don't use end tags...which is how I figured out the issue (I made the same initial assumption you did)

![mitmproxy_screenshot](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1291387/41494068-5646039a-7106-11e8-8767-5cec5af3ec85.png)

I also used mitmproxy to inspect how ofxtools was actually communicating with BECU and to try to get it to match how Banktivity did it, which is when I ran into the end tags issue described above.

I think the easiest way to verify this is by running the following:

curl -v -X POST --data-binary @ofx.txt --header "Content-Type: application/x-ofx" --header "Accept:*/*, application/x-ofx" https://onlinebanking.becu.org/ofx/ofxprocessor.asp

Swap out _bad and _good for...bad and good results. The _only_ difference is the ending tags:

[ofx_bad.txt](https://github.com/csingley/ofxtools/files/2107745/ofx_bad.txt)
[ofx_good.txt](https://github.com/csingley/ofxtools/files/2107746/ofx_good.txt)

I've read your thoughts, and there are some points I already thought of.

* I've been playing with Reddit's **implicit grant** flow. This one makes the OAuth process implementation easier, but as you said, it doesn't support permanent tokens, which is a no-go to me. This brings up the "web browser" issue. I understand that you dislike having to use a web browser, I've been thinking about this as well. I found that using the **code** flow kind of limits the damage, since the web browser is used only once. Implicit grant flow would require web browser each time the token expires, that is every hour. So my opinion is that the **code** flow is the best option.

* I agree with you about the third-party web server. In fact, I did set it up for myself, and I'm not sure it is a good idea to deploy OAuth in the main RTV with my webserver. In this regard, I tried to make it easy to edit the OAuth app settings (in config.py). On the other hand, using a stand-alone webserver could be a security issue, as it is not using HTTPS, unencrypted OAuth data would transit through Reddit servers and user's PC. So unless we configure SSL on the stand-alone server, we have to keep that mind as well.

* Providing a small interface to Reddit's authorization page could be a possibility. I haven't thought of this yet, we could discuss this one. But as you said, it's quite hacky, and to me it feels like it will complicate things. If you ask me, I prefer having to click "Accept" on a web browser once, but this is only my opinion.

* I haven't thought of that either. Now in the current setup, logging in as a different user would need one to logout from his web browser, login as the other user, and redo the procedure. Now this is indeed not ideal. Maybe asking for username and password would be a good idea.

You can `except SystemExit as e:` if you don't want it to quit, and then check `if e.code == 0` to see if everything succeeded. Pip calls `sys.exit()` for some reason, and runpy simply doesn't catch that for you.

Of course, future versions of pip don't necessarily `sys.exit()`. Python exits with status 0 by default, so if the `except SystemExit` block doesn't run, everything succeeded.

@Valerievich Anyway, it's cool.

The code to pull data from the `backfill_from` source is dead simple:
```python
                if not self.p.backfill_from.next():
                    # additional data source is consumed
                    self._state = self._ST_START
                    continue

                # copy lines of the same name
                for alias in self.lines.getlinealiases():
                    lsrc = getattr(self.p.backfill_from.lines, alias)
                    ldst = getattr(self.lines, alias)

                    ldst[0] = lsrc[0]

                return True
```

It won't stop until the data source is exhausted. The options to consider:

  1. The `backfill_from` has only data to the point shown. This should not be the case because you have checked the actual content.

  2. The resampling code is getting confused at some point and fails to deliver that last bar.

  3. The `datetime` interactions are confusing

And after checking the code it may be that number `3` is the winner. In `GenericCSVData` the following conversion takes place

```python
        self.lines.datetime[0] = date2num(dt)
```

This after using the `sessionend=dt.time(16, 0)` that you have specified. Because the high level `date2num` function is being used, rather than the method available in each data feed, `16:00` is being understood as `UTC` time.

There is another parameter to each data feed which is `tzinput`, to convert time if coming from already in a timezone specific format. Although it would actually help in this case it isn't the route to go.

Some of the original goals may have been too ambitious, simply by not forcing users to specify things like timeframes (and by python not having a time concept like `end of day`) A large thoughtful process has to take place.

@kyuupichan this is a general problem with various coins (older codebases) where you cannot get the coinbase transaction from an orphan block as it is not indexed (so I run patched versions). Another (rare) scenario is when you have 2 daemons and when switching the other node is on a different chain.

A solution could be storing a `REORG_LIMIT` number of latest blocks and using those then reorging instead of asking the daemon. The blocks could be saved as files, with the block height as the filename and be regularly deleted every time the blockchain advances.

More progress. As [suggested][1] on Stackoverflow, the problem is with an installed script that fails to have necessary quotes in the shebang line.  Specifically, for my current python installation under Windows 10, `C:\Python 35-32\Scripts\pandoc-eqnos-script.py` has the following first line:

     #!c:\python 35-32\python.exe

Changing this to 

     #!"c:\python 35-32\python.exe"

fixes the problem.

Note that there is also a `pandoc-eqnos.exe` in the `Scripts` directory.  When I compare this to my Windows 7 system, there is no `.py` script, just the `.exe`.  This is presumably why the Issue only happens in Windows 10.

I will try to find a way to remedy this problem in the install script.

[1]: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/31808180/installing-pyinstaller-via-pip-leads-to-failed-to-create-process

According to CFFI docs http://cffi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/ref.html#ffi-gc

> Note that ffi.gc() should be avoided for large memory allocations or for limited resources. This is particularly true on PyPy: its GC does not know how much memory or how many resources the returned ptr holds. It will only run its GC when enough memory it knows about has been allocated (and thus run the destructor possibly later than you would expect). Moreover, the destructor is called in whatever thread PyPy is at that moment, which might be a problem for some C libraries. In these cases, consider writing a wrapper class with custom __enter__() and __exit__() methods, allocating and freeing the C data at known points in time, and using it in a with statement.

I think it's not a memory leak, but rather PyPy doesn't run GC yet.
I will follow the note, get rid of ffi.gc, and see if it can alleviate the memory occupation problem.

We don't need to transfer the IP address of the user.
Instead, we can have a network of rendez-vous servers, that know about each other (hold a list of other servers, join new servers to this list).
A client bootstraps into this network and downloads a list of servers. Then, it picks one at random, performs the usual rendez-vous stuff. It gets its one-time password, then compresses the IP of the server, and the resulting string looks something like AzS9Sf4-purple-sausages.
There's still an infrastructure, yes, but it's peer-to-peer, it's self-configuring, it costs peanuts to run.

I assume that you are using aiohttp to create web server, that expose some endpoint.
And endpoint "/serp_top/" actually make request to external service "https://api.vertifire.com".
I'm correct ?

If so, then you have to split your test.

`aiohttp.test_utils.TestClient` object (self.client in test) under the neath uses the `ClientSession` to perform request to your web server. What aioresponses does is it mocks some method in the `ClientSession` class. So it's not possible or harder to combine aioresponses together with aiohttp  webserver in test. `aioresponses` was not designed to cooperate with `aiohttp.web.Application`

What you can do is to have two methods: first method `serp_rop_request_handler `  will be request handler for "/serp_top/" in your web server and the second one `get_top_api` that actually hit external server.

example below

```python

# web.py
from path.to.vertifire import get_top_api

async def serp_rop_request_handler(request):
    top_data = await get_top_api()
    return web.Response(data=top_data)

app = web.Application()
app.router.add_get('/serp_top/', serp_rop_request_handler)


# test_web.py
# ///    and test for it in
@unittest_run_loop
@patch('path.to.vertifire.get_top_api')
async def test_serp_top(self, mocked):
    # note that get_top_api is a coroutine so mock object must also return coroutine
    mocked.side_effect = asyncio.coroutine(lambda *args, **kwargs: dict(data="it could be anything"))

    request = await self.client.request("GET", "/serp_top/")
    assert request.status == 200
    response_data = await request.json()
    assert "data" in response_data
```
And method and test that gets data from external service

```python
# vertifire.py

async def get_top_api():
    # todo: try except in case of 404 or 500 or connection refused....
    session = ClientSession()
    resp = await session.get(VERTIFIRE_SERP_TOP_API_URL)
    # process response and extract data
    resp.raise_for_status()   
    data = await resp.json()
    return data


# test_vertifire.py

async def test_correct_response_from_verfire(self):
    with aioresponses() as m:
        m.get(VERTIFIRE_SERP_TOP_API_URL, status=200, payload=dict(data='some data'))
       
        result = await get_top_api()

async def test_bad_response_from_verfire(self):
    with aioresponses() as m:
        m.get(VERTIFIRE_SERP_TOP_API_URL, status=404)
        with self.assertRaises( ClientResponseError ):
            await get_top_api()

```

So at the end you do not have to use `passthrough` since it's unittest and you decide what should be returned by external service in your tests.

I hope it helps you.
In case of further questions feel free to ask them :)

The reason is that it first try the unicode case, and if it can not convert it, send a representation of the object in a string (I guess it is for debugging purpose) doing the repr of a numpy array is ok because it is truncated, but if for some reason we send a list, it is going to do a repr of a list which could be 1000x1000x3 (It happend to me for the color because I did not convert it to a numpy array). If you are using new style middleware classes using `MIDDLEWARE` instead of `MIDDLEWARE_CLASSES` setting, then you will see this issue.  This is because django stronghold does not yet support the new style middleware. See #53.

As a word around you should be able to do something like this (rough code, untested):
```python
from django.utils.deprecation import MiddlewareMixin
from stronghold import LoginRequiredMiddleware

class MyLoginRequiredMiddleware(MiddlewareMixin, LoginRequiredMiddleware):
    pass
```
Then use this class instead of `strong.LoginRequiredMiddleware` in your `MIDDLEWARE` list in Django settings.

If you want a common prefix, you can simply register the API on a Blueprint:
```python
from flask import Flask, Blueprint
from flask.ext.restplus import Api

app = Flask(__name__)
blueprint = Blueprint('api', __name__, url_prefix='/api') 
api = Api(blueprint)

app.register_blueprint(blueprint)
```
All your endpoints will be prefixed by '``api.``' and all your URLs by ``/api``.

> Regardless of whether you define a media declaration, all Form objects have a media property. The default value for this property is the result of adding the media definitions for all widgets that are part of the form

@elpirato83 it is time to test the GATE-03 sensor support! Please get the three python files changed from this PR: https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant/pull/11994 and place them in your HASS install folder in the folder called `custom_components` (create it if it does not exist). Make sure to place the files in correct subfolders of the folder: i.e. the generic component (https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant/pull/11994/files#diff-1f2ba323864a7b7698b7e6b9a8926a90) goes into the `custom_components` folder. The binary sensor file goes into `custom_components/binary_sensor` (file: https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant/pull/11994/files#diff-798eeca65292c544122e9e6125cd8da6). The alarm control panel file goes into `custom_components/alarm_control_panel` (file: https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant/pull/11994/files#diff-703c178c3f0f061c6bc54389dcdbac96). You will need to make one last change, and that includes changing your `configuration.yaml`. You will need to get rid of the explicit egardia as alarm_control_panel. Instead use this (include whatever you normally have):
```yaml
egardia:
    host: YOUR_HOST
    username: YOUR_USERNAME
    password: YOUR_PASSWORD
```

Thank you for your comments. I agree partially with them:

1. There should be a factor of 2 e^2 in front of the expression for polarizability in example notebook: http://arc-alkali-rydberg-calculator.readthedocs.io/en/latest/_static/Rydberg_atoms_a_primer.html#Rydberg-Atom-Stark-Shifts
2. However, there should not be the factor of 1/3. This is different compared to the cited eq. (2.8) from the linked thesis because they are doing there averaging over orientations of the molecule. Here, we consider atoms whose quantization axis is directed along the electric field (q=0). Therefore, we don't include all mj2, just mj2 = mj1, and sum only over n2, l2

In your example if you add factor of 2 in calculation (to correct for 1.) and don't do averaging over all mj2, but use only mj2 = mj1 (corresponding to q=0; electric field directed along the quantiazation axis), you get that calculated result and result returned by the program match. The coresponding correction of your code is:

```
atom=Rubidium()
pol2=0.
l2=1
mj2=mj1 # ISSUE 2: fixed orientation, E field along quantization axis
q = 0
for n2 in range(n1-10,n1+10):
    for j2 in [0.5,1.5]:
        energy_difference = ( atom.getEnergy(n2,l2,j2) - atom.getEnergy(n1,l1,j1)) / 27.2114    # convert eV to Hartree
        dipole_matrix_element = atom.getDipoleMatrixElement(n1,l1,j1,mj1,n2,l2,j2,mj2,q)
        # ISSUE 1: added factor of 2
        pol2 += 2*dipole_matrix_element**2 / energy_difference
pol2_au = pol2
pol2 = (pol2)*polarisability_atomic_units/h*1e4/1e6

print("polarisability calculated from dipole matrix elements")
print("%.2f MHz cm^2 / V^2" % (pol2))
print("%.2e atomic units  " % (pol2_au))
print
```

I will correct the equation in example notebook and close this issue. Thank you for spotting this mistake!

Just to clarify for other readers: polarizabilities from ARC were correct (as they are checked with experimental values), but the question is the equation in the notebook.

The summary of this issue (thanks @xinyu-intel) :

https://mran.microsoft.com/documents/rro/multithread 
On Linux platforms, Microsoft R Open users can enable multi-threaded performance by downloading the optional, custom Intel Math Kernel Library (call mini R MKL and only for R). This library will install the custom RevoUtilsMath package into the default search path and provides the BLAS and LAPACK library functions.

But it’s impossible (or not the recommended usage) to export this MKL bundled with MRO to MXNET as a complete BLAS. 

So, the best way is to install the Intel MKL in your system for MXNET BLAS.

The reason the ``Status`` was not included in the response dictionary was that Auto Scaling server did not include it in its response. Also if you scroll down past the ``Request Syntax`` section in the ``Response Structure`` section, it states the following for ``Status``:

So my guess is Auto Scaling will only include ``Status`` if you are deleting the group.

After looking at the API docs, I was not seeing an element that will always give the state of the autoscaling group (and not have to be deleting). To determine if the autoscaling group is up and running, I would probably check the ``HealthStatus`` of the instances in the autoscaling group.

I think it's important to consider IDE and human readability implications of the Pipfile. In terms of IDE, if it's a Python-like syntax, the IDE (or text editor like Sublime) would have to embed an interpreter if it doesn't already have one, and would have to hope that the syntax stays valid for the foreseeable future (if, for example, there was a bug due to different Python versions, an end user would be very confused if their IDE gave them errors). This is probably not much of a concern, but something to be aware of. I think one big benefit to making the Pipfile easily parseable is that an IDE could infer requirements and alert the user that they're attempting to require something that isn't in their Pipfile. 

In terms of human readability, I know this may be contentious but I believe that a config file like Pipfile should be easily human readable and I don't think having them use a Python like syntax would accomplish this. It would mean another syntax to something that's already been solved over and over (YAML, TOML, INI...) and would just be another overhead to users who want to develop in Python on a bigger project. The Pipfile.lock can be in whatever format necessary because end users aren't going to really be touching it, but the Pipfile should be easily edited. 

Whichever config language is chosen, I think choosing a standard one like TOML or YAML would really benefit not only programmers but also project maintainers who will have to deal with Pipfile issues over the course of the project lifetime.

Also, off topic: please make importing from a git repo dead simple. I love that in `package.json`, you just point it to a Github repo and it basically works, but in `requirements.txt` you have to prepend the repo with `-e`and add some egg info for it to not try to install each time you do install using `requirements.txt`. It's a minor concern but because I push to CI multiple times per day, that installation time matters and onboarding someone who doesn't know about the `-e` flag and needs to import a private library can be headache prone and cause wasted time during CI and deployments.

I had a chance to look through the code and the reference

The issue comes from the use of `arctan`:
https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/d1f048e1b55f64865cd3eba4a2b08c1680426f6a/skimage/feature/corner.py#L421

Unfortunately, the way the `shape` is defined, it is difficult (for me) to write a close form formula that uses some combination of `arctan2`, `modulo` or whatever else that avoids the use of the division operator. The reason is that the shape function is defined on a closed interval (in math notation) $$s \in [-1, 1]$$ and I can't figure out a clever way to make the C code give something in closed interval that I can map to the correct answer. It originates from the fact that `-0` and `+0`are different for IEEE, as are `-inf` and `+inf`.

Finally, currently the computation of the angle is order "cost of 1 division" and "cost of arctan". I wouldn't want to blow that up significantly for an edge case that is correctly handled by `arctan`

For reference, here is something you can use to visually check for correctness if you think of something smart:

```python
import numpy as np
from numpy import pi

the_sum = np.array([-4, -4, 0, 0, 4, 4])
the_diff = np.zeros(the_sum.shape)
negative_zero = -np.zeros(1)
the_diff[::2] = negative_zero  # -0 and +0 are actually different!!!

the_diff_corrected = the_diff
the_diff_corrected[the_diff > 0] *= -1

print('arctan2  : ' + str(np.arctan2(the_sum, the_diff)/pi))
print('arctan   : ' + str(np.arctan(the_sum/the_diff)/pi))
print('expected : ' + '[ 0.5 -0.5  dc   dc  -0.5  0.5]')
print('zero fun : ' + str(the_sum/the_diff))
print('         : ' + str(1 * the_diff))
print('         : ' + str(np.sign(the_diff)))
print("dc is don't care or some 0 value")
```

@stefanv I don't think the function name provides any indication of "I'm going to shoot you if your range is incorrect". For that I would use something like `ensure_float_range`. Our position with range conversions should be to raise an error when absolutely necessary and no earlier. This function currently raises an error way too early, in a place that is unexpected by most of our users (as numerous bug reports stemming from this behaviour show).
This doesn't seem to be an skimage issue, so I'm closing for now, but feel free to re-open if there's any proof to the contrary.

I'm -1 on this change. This will lead to a bunch of confusion for users. See [Python 3 Statement: Practicalities](https://python3statement.org/practicalities/) under "Fail early at import time". Feel free to move this into a utility function though, `assert_valid_python3_version()`. Actually we should check for Python >= 3.5, not just 3...

@Megh-Thakkar I'm not going to be of much help, but if you are having discrepancies between `rgb2hsv` in matlab and scikit-image it might be due to the way `scikit-image` and matlab compute them.

I ran into this myself a while back Issue #3058

My first tip would be: make sure you images are floats in both scikit-image and matlab. There are strange things that can happen with implicit conversions. You may choose to go back to `uint8` after you figure out the peculiarities of the color conversion.

Since you are rather mathematical, go through the code for [rgb2hsv](https://github.com/scikit-image/scikit-image/blob/master/skimage/color/colorconv.py#L224) and make sure it is performing the color conversion you are expecting. Finally, go through it for [matlab](https://www.mathworks.com/help/matlab/ref/rgb2hsv.html) as well, though that one is harder to do since it is likely closed source ;).

La mort oui, avec un attribut de variable comme `end = '2012-12-31'`.

> Par contre, on aimerait savoir qu'une variable d''input (donc sans formula) n'existait pas avant une certaine date et donc qu'elle renvoie une erreur ou passe encore prenne sa valeur par défaut si elle est appelée avant cette date.

Dans le comportement actuel, si une variable n'a pas de formule, alors quand on la calcule pour une période:
- Soit un input a été défini, et alors on obtient cet input
- Soit aucun input n'est défini pour la période, et on obtient la valeur par défaut

Donc en soit, il semble que "pas de start date" est équivalent à "start date" + deuxième comportement que tu proposes. À moins que tu ne suggères que la variable retourne la valeur par défaut _même si_ l'utilisateur a défini un input, ce qui me paraît assez contre-intuitif. 

Sur le premier comportement proposé (renvoyer une erreur) pourquoi pas, mais:
- A minima, il faudra aussi renvoyer une erreur si on définit une entrée pour une variable _après_ sa date de fin
- Si on va dans ce sens, est-ce qu'on souhaite aussi que le calcul d'une variable après sa date de fin (et potentiellement avant sa date de début) renvoie une erreur plutôt que la valeur par défaut ? 
    - Si non, on a un comportement inhomogène qui peut être un peu contre-intuitif: j'ai le droit de calculer F74B en 2014, mais pas le droit de la définir en input ? Cette inhomogéniété peut être justifiée, mais il faudrait dans ce cas que la doc soit bien claire sur la question.
    - Si oui, ça veut dire qu'il faut être plus strict dans la rédaction des formules. Quitte à multiplier les dated formulas qui se ressemblent : par exemple si la PAJE disparaît en 2016, alors je dois introduire une nouvelle formule pour _toutes_ les variables qui dépendent de la PAJE, nouvelle formule qui est probablement un copier-coller de l'ancienne à une ligne près, alors qu'avant je pouvais me reposer implicitement sur les valeurs par défauts. Est-ce que c'est dans cette direction qu'on veut aller ? Je n'ai pas forcément d'objection absolue, mais il faut que tout le monde soit bien au clair sur les conséquences.
- Pour les cas d'usages type application web, il faut qu'on soit un minimum flexibles pour éviter les problèmes de productions qui arrivent du jour au lendemain. Donc si les simulations type IPP demandent un mode "strict", il faudra être capable de switcher en mode "flexible" pour l'API Web. C'est possible et justifié si les deux cas d'usages ont des exigences incompatibles, mais ça amène son lot de complexité de potentiels ennuis.

class aide_logement_montant_brut_crds(Variable):
    value_type = float
    entity = Famille
    label = u"Montant des aides au logement net de RLS et CRDS"
    reference = u"https://www.legifrance.gouv.fr/eli/decret/2018/2/27/2018-136/jo/article_1"
    definition_period = MONTH
    def formula_2018(famille, period, parameters):
        rls = parameters(period).prestations.reduction_loyer_solidarite
        aide_logement_montant_brut = famille('aide_logement_montant_brut', period)
        reduction_loyer_solidarite = famille('reduction_loyer_solidarite', period)
        statut_occupation_logement = famille.demandeur.menage('statut_occupation_logement', period)

        taux_rls = rls.fraction_baisse_aide_logement
        rls_apl = reduction_loyer_solidarite * taux_rls * (
                statut_occupation_logement == TypesStatutOccupationLogement.locataire_hlm)
        montant = max_(0, (aide_logement_montant_brut - rls_apl))
        return montant


- modifie le calcul de cdrs_logement
class crds_logement(Variable):
    calculate_output = calculate_output_add
    value_type = float
    entity = Famille
    label = u"CRDS des allocations logement"
    reference = u"http://vosdroits.service-public.fr/particuliers/F17585.xhtml"
    definition_period = MONTH

    def formula(famille, period, parameters):
        aide_logement_montant_brut_crds = famille('aide_logement_montant_brut_crds', period)
        crds = parameters(period).prestations.prestations_familiales.af.crds
        return -aide_logement_montant_brut_crds * crds

Py-ART will now decompress gzip and bzip2 files passed to the `read_nexrad_archive` function.  Adding this functionality directly to the **NEXRADLevel2File** and related classes would introduced undesired coupling in this module or require extensive repetition of code, but of which are bad.  Py-ART does expose the `prepare_for_read` function in the pyart.io namespace which can be used to add this functionality to the **NEXRADLevel2File** or other classes.  For example:

``` Python
import pyart
from pyart.io.nexrad_level2 import NEXRADLevel2File
nfile = NEXRADLevel2File(pyart.io.prepare_for_read('KATX20130717_195021_V06.gz'))
```

Additionally the `prepare_for_read` function can be easily exacted from Py-ART and included directly in the nexrad_level2.py module if so desired.

I believe this to be the best solution to this issue, although it is not exactly what was requested.

@kirknorth thanks for your thoughts. Was playing around with gatefilters yesterday similar to what you were proposing. It did improve things in that cell close to the radar, but the Bringi Kdp filter as implemented by @tjlang was handling the cell better, primarily because it has a standard deviation of phase filter that removes those phase outliers. Yes, you are right that there is clutter, and insects, etc. contributing to noisey phase values -- I should have been more specific. Also, it appears that we are battling the clutter filters as well in meteorological echo. I put together an illustration that shows the issues - you can see where the phase values don't follow the propagation norm occur in small targets like clutter, but also near the 0 isodop in precipitation, and sometimes in precipitation (could be other ground targets, although they occur at wide ranges of rho_hv, Z, and spectral width). ![movie](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/3288592/14385506/89a72e14-fd66-11e5-913d-526853251da1.gif) Seems like a standard deviation of phase filter (or other noise filter) could be useful in tossing out outliers that we shouldn't be concerned about. The Bringi filter removes all gates in the filter where the standard deviation may be affected by an outlier, but it seems we could be smarter about that to preserve more information. Kalman filters can handle this to some extent as long as you teach them to ignore those outliers.

@arvindnrbt *with consulting hat on* "It depends...".  You could:

- Iterate and upload files one by one.
- Populate a `queue.Queue` with the filenames and have worker threads upload items from the queue.
- Populate a cross-process queue and have worker processes upload items from the queue.

You can't:

- Upload a zipfile and have GCS automagically explode its entries into blobs / objects.
- Create a ["batched" MIME request](https://cloud.google.com/storage/docs/json_api/v1/how-tos/batch) and do multiple uploads in a single request.

We enable this behavior as part of the configuration for the network setup daemon. We have some documentation in our [README](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages#configuration) you may find helpful.

In a user config template file, you can specify a hook script to call in place of our network setup logic:

```
/etc/default/instance_configs.cfg.template
[NetworkInterfaces]
dhclient_script = {YOUR_DHCLIENT_HOOK_SCRIPT}
```

Alternatively, you can disable the network setup daemon from running at all and do everything yourself using the config options:
```
[NetworkInterfaces]
setup = false
```

If you don't want to write your own script, but need to call a different DHCP client, you can use:
```
[NetworkInterfaces]
dhcp_command = {YOUR_DHCP_CMD}
```

If you end up writing your own script, you may find it helpful to refer to the existing [network setup logic](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/compute-image-packages/blob/master/google_compute_engine/network_utils.py) we currently use.

I think throwing the `KeyError: "can't get value of empty TimeSeries with no default value"` might be OK here. If you want to avoid the error, I'd recommend:

```python
ts = TimeSeries()
if not ts.is_floating():
    print(ts.default)
```

`is_floating` is similar to something like `has_no_default` in that it only returns `True` if it's an empty TimeSeries with no default value specified.

The bug should be against the RDS usage instead of the S3 usage. The intent is to allow the following usage to make it easier to format Tags:
```
>>> from troposphere import Tags
>>> Tags(foo='bar', baz='boo').JSONrepr()
[{'Value': 'boo', 'Key': 'baz'}, {'Value': 'bar', 'Key': 'foo'}]
```
or
```
>>> from troposphere import Tags
>>> d = {'foo': 'bar', 'baz': 'boo'}
>>> Tags(**d).JSONrepr()
[{'Value': 'boo', 'Key': 'baz'}, {'Value': 'bar', 'Key': 'foo'}]
```

The easiest way to do this is to amend the last commit:
~~~console
$ git clone -b "support-tcp-upgrade" git@github.com:aanand/docker-py.git somewhere
$ cd somewhere
$ git rebase -i HEAD~12
editor opens
change each 'pick' to 'edit'
save the file and quit
$ git commit --amend -s --no-edit
$ git rebase --continue # and repeat the amend for each commit
$ git push -f
~~~

As one cannot throw away all messages arrived before processing the current state I see problems with the `wamp.registration` API.

In my app, I added a "message sequence number" which is increased for every package sent. Clients can then drop all packages with a lower sequence number. This is not possible with the given API though.

@meejah the handshake is describe here https://github.com/wamp-proto/wamp-proto/blob/master/rfc/draft-oberstet-hybi-crossbar-wamp.txt#L6837

Essentially, the first, third and forth octet of both the request and reply are fixed (7F and 00 00).

So it's all about the 2nd octet.

The lower 4 bits are the serializer ID. Client requests are serializer, router either denies or must use this serializer and echo the bits.

The upper 4 bits are the interesting part.

The client can request a maximum length of messages it is willing to process, and the router must not send messages any longer than that.

The router _also_ requests a maximum length, and this is _independent_ of the client requested maximum length.

Actually, looking at both the asyncio and Twisted implementation, I don't think this behavior is implemented.

So to gather requirements here with an example:

 - We have an SSL certificate with common name `foo.deisapp.com`
 - A certificate contains SAN entries `bar.deisapp.com`, `car.deisapp.com`, `star.deisapp.com`
 - `deis certs:add <cert>` by default should attach all SAN entries
 - if `--subjaltnames bar.deisapp.com,car.deisapp.com` is supplied, only those two alt names are read in
 - after creating the certificate, no other certificate may claim those names

In that case, this could be an easy PR by just creating additional Certificate objects in the database for each subject alt name. We already have the infrastructure in the controller for this, so it'd just be a client change. This has the effect of having to manually delete each entry for each SAN, but for some users this may be a benefit. Then again, SSL is not my strong point.

I think that consistency comes at the expense of good semantics for each command and it is unwise to carry it into the new semantic territory of `conda env`.

If a user knows that `conda create` really means `conda create_env`, then it is obvious that `--name` refers to the name of the env being created. However, consider `conda package --name`. A reasonable person reading seeing that command without knowing that it is an analogy to `conda create` could easily guess that `--name` referred to the name of a package, not the name of an environment. Or `conda update --name`, which could refer to the name of a something to be updated to or from. Or `conda install --name`, which could refer to an alias that you want to give the newly installed package. For all of these commands besides `conda create`, a better name for that option would be `--env`. The meaning of `conda install pygment --env tundra` (or `conda install pygment --env /afs/lab-common/tundra`) is slightly more clear than `conda install pygment --name tundra. 

Is it worth changing things now? Maybe not. A lot of old shell scripts would need to be edited on a lot of user machines.
But `conda env` is conceptually a new space whose users want to deal primarily with manipulating environments and there is no reason to carry the `--name` convention onto what is currently free soil. A user typing `conda env update` knows that they are dealing with environments and expects to give the name of an environment.

I did some digging, and the long and short of it is that Python will not let you use hours in a timestring to extract a timestamp. I know, it was a huge surprise to me, and I'll be having to document this.  You're the first to report it (lucky you, right?).

I had used a hacky way of getting around this in the Curator 2.x and 3.x era. However, this is no longer a recommended approach, since a much better way of calculating age now exists in Elasticsearch.

You can still use this timestring with `%H` hours to filter indices like this:
```
- filtertype: pattern
  kind: timestring
  value: '%Y.%m.%d.%H'
```
Since you cannot use this timestring to calculate age, I recommend using [`source: creation_date`](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/fe_source.html#_creation_date), or if you've been ingesting old data and the creation date would be inaccurate, the most accurate is 
 [`source: field_stats`](https://www.elastic.co/guide/en/elasticsearch/client/curator/current/fe_source.html#_field_stats).  

`creation_date` is simply the epoch timestamp recorded at the time the index was created.  With time-series data this is usually accurate, but it can be inaccurate if you've ingested some old syslog line, and Logstash creates an index for `2017.01.01` on the 20th of the month. Based on the _content_ of the log line, Logstash makes the index name's date in the past, but the `creation_date` would still be `2017.01.20`.  To avoid scenarios like this, use `source: field_stats` to calculate index age.

The `field_stats` API in Elasticsearch will tell you what the min and max values of a field are in an Elasticsearch index.  For Curator, presuming you're using the `@timestamp` field, the configuration might look like:

```
# '@timestamp' is the default value for 'field', so the 'field' line can be omitted if that is the case
# 'min_value' is the default for 'stats_result', so the 'stats_result' line can be omitted if that is the case
- filtertype: age
  source: field_stats
  field: '@timestamp'
  stats_result: min_value 
  direction: older
  unit: days
  unit_count: 3
```
This will calculate an index's age based on the minimum value found for `@timestamp` in the index.  If that is older than 3 days ago, it will remain in the actionable list.  

You could also use `max_value` for `stats_result`, which would calculate index age based on the "newest" value in the index.  It is important to remember that for time calculation, `min_value` and `max_value` are going to be evaluating epoch time.  As such, a bigger value indicates a more recent time stamp.

@oakeyc this is great! I would suggest a couple things from a usability perspective:

1. The script identifies missing parameter and command help, but not subgroup. You should also identify those.
2. Instead of just dumping to STDOUT, it might be more useful to structure the results in a JSON object. A structure might look like:
```
"groups": [
  "az vm"
  ...
],
"commands": [
   "az vm wait",
   "az network nic wait",
   ...
],
"params": {
   "az vm create": [
     "sku",
     ...
   ],
   "az network nic create": [
      "foo",
      ...
   ]
}
```
In this way, the results are 1) more readable 2) more informative and 3) programmatically useful. We would be able to more easily use this data structure and weave it into a CI script or something to catch PRs that are missing help, for example.

I agree with @xtophs  here, especially since most open source software built for OAuth interactions use the terms `client_id` and `client_secret`.

In ACS-Engine, @xtophs was trying to write better documentation about the Service Principal process... and we realize that it's much wordy than it needs to be because we have to specifically explain what each field is. `appId` and `password` aren't going to look familiar to anyone who isn't pretty intimately familiar with the workings of AAD.

In my opinion, this would be an improvement.

{
"**clientId**: "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000",
"**name**": "http://some-name-that-is-also-an-app-uri-identifier",
"**clientSecret**": "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000",
"tenant**Id**": "00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000"
}

pytest-asyncio is not fully compatible with pytest-aiohttp (while last pytest-asyncio commits has smoothed some corners).

The crucial difference is disabling global event loop by default in pytest-aiohttp.
Another difference is `event_loop` vs `loop` fixture name. From my perspective `loop` is more native: it's used widely by asyncio itself, no `event_loop` variable in asyncio code.

After `asyncio.get_event_loop()` fix in Python 3.5.3+ default event loop issue becomes negligible but it's still very important until aiohttp will drop older Python versions support (I hope we'll do it it after next release).

I don't believe it's possible to get the right answer every time, or even most of the time, inside a container because the container doesn't know what port or ip is accessible from the outside:

- the port may not match (and typically doesn't), and the actual accessible port cannot be retrieved from inside the container unless we add something explicit like a `PUBLIC_PORT` or `PUBLIC_URL` env.
- the ip may not match, and there are lots of different right answers, depending on context not available inside the container:
  - localhost generally works for `--net host` and local docker
  - docker ip is right if docker network is local (not with docker-machine)
  - with docker-machine, the `docker-machine ip` is what should be used, not localhost or any local ip discoverable from inside the container

The only general solution, I think, is to have a "connect_host" config that overrides the connectable address to be echoed here. It should include both the hostname and port in order to handle the fact that the port cannot be known from inside the container. This would have to come from the user manually at start time. With that, we could have:

    docker run --rm -e JUPYTER_CONNECT_HOST=http://$(docker-machine ip mymachine):9999 -p9999:8888 -it jupyter/base-notebook

echo the correct URL:

    http://192.168.99.100:9999/?token=...

From the utilities we already have, we can change the default something like what @BradenAtSiemens proposes, by echoing the first or all of `jupyter_client.localinterfaces.public_ips()`, which gives

    ['172.17.0.3']

in a docker container. However, for me (on macOS), this ip does *not* work, only localhost does, so we could either show *both* this ip and localhost, or just go back to using localhost in this case.

In testing ``planemo dependency_script`` on https://github.com/peterjc/pico_galaxy the dependencies are failing for https://github.com/peterjc/pico_galaxy/tree/master/tools/clinod and https://github.com/peterjc/pico_galaxy/tree/master/tools/effectiveT3 which I traced to the assumption in their ``tool_dependencies.xml`` files that the current directory *is* ``$INSTALL_DIR``:

```xml
<actions>
    <!-- Set environment variable $CLINOD so wrapper knows where to look -->
    <action type="set_environment">
        <environment_variable name="CLINOD" action="set_to">$INSTALL_DIR</environment_variable>
    </action>
    <!-- clinod requires the SNNS Batch Interpreter v1.0 executable -->
    <action type="shell_command">wget http://www.ra.cs.uni-tuebingen.de/downloads/SNNS/SNNSv4.3.tar.gz</action>
    <action type="shell_command">tar -zxvf SNNSv4.3.tar.gz</action>
    <action type="move_file"><source>SNNSv4.3/tools/bin/x86_64-pc-unknown-linux-gnuoldld/batchman</source><destination>$INSTALL_DIR/</destination></action>
    <!-- clinod itself is just a JAR file -->
    <action type="shell_command">wget http://www.compbio.dundee.ac.uk/nod/downloads/clinod-1.3.jar</action>
    <!-- Don't need to move it, $INSTALL_DIR is the current directory
    <action type="move_file"><source>clinod-1.3.jar</source><destination>$INSTALL_DIR</destination></action>
    -->
 </actions>
```

Currently the ``dep_install.sh`` script produced from this is:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#set -euo pipefail
set -eo pipefail
if [[ ! -d $INSTALL_DIR ]]
then
    echo "ERROR: Environment variable INSTALL_DIR not a directory!"
    exit 1
fi
# Set full strict mode now, side stepping case $INSTALL_DIR not setup.
set -euo pipefail
export DOWNLOAD_CACHE=`(cd "$DOWNLOAD_CACHE"; pwd)`
if [[ ! -d $DOWNLOAD_CACHE ]]
then
    mkdir -p $DOWNLOAD_CACHE
fi
echo "Using $DOWNLOAD_CACHE for cached downloads."
export INSTALL_DIR=`(cd "$INSTALL_DIR"; pwd)`
echo "Using $INSTALL_DIR for the installed files."
# Create a randomly named temp folder for working in
dep_install_tmp=${TMPDIR-/tmp}/dep_install.$RANDOM.$RANDOM.$RANDOM.$$
(umask 077 && mkdir $dep_install_tmp) || exit 1
echo "============================================================"
echo "Installing clinod version 1.3"
echo "============================================================"
specifc_action_done=0
cd $dep_install_tmp
#------------------------------------------------------------
if [[ $specifc_action_done == 0 ]]
then
    echo "Non-platform-specific actions"
    export CLINOD=$INSTALL_DIR
    wget http://www.ra.cs.uni-tuebingen.de/downloads/SNNS/SNNSv4.3.tar.gz
    tar -zxvf SNNSv4.3.tar.gz
    mv SNNSv4.3/tools/bin/x86_64-pc-unknown-linux-gnuoldld/batchman $INSTALL_DIR/
    wget http://www.compbio.dundee.ac.uk/nod/downloads/clinod-1.3.jar
fi
echo "Cleaning up..."
rm -rf $dep_install_tmp
echo "======================"
echo "Installation complete."
echo "======================"
```

By deliberately creating a randomly named temporary working directory this means that in this case while the binary ``batchman`` is moved into ``$INSTALL_DIR``, the jar file ``clinod-1.3.jar`` is left in the temporary folder.

```

Here the four modules are explicitly moved into ``$INSTALL_DIR/module`` but the master JAR file ``TTSS_GUI-1.0.1.jar`` is assumed to be at ``$INSTALL_DIR/TTSS_GUI-1.0.1.jar`` but is currently left in the temporary working folder.

Judging from my comments in the XML, I was surprised that when ``tool_dependencies.xml`` is executed by the Tool Shed installation ``$INSTALL_DIR`` is the current working directory. I would have thought a temporary working folder would make more sense (no reason to keep temporary files generated during compilation for example). Evidently I was still thinking this when writing the ``planemo dependency_script`` command.

It seems the ``planemo dependency_script`` output needs to include a ``cd $INSTALL_DIR`` line and abandon my temporary working directory approach for full compatibility with existing ``tool_dependencies.xml`` files? Given this, as per the original goal of this issue, we ought to use a separate ``$INSTALL_DIR`` for each ``tool_dependencies.xml`` file.

My initial reaction is that I'd prefer to avoid taking this sort of dependency inside peru itself, if we could handle the same sort of use case with external tools. Would it be possible to handle your workflow with a [Watchman trigger](https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/cmd/trigger.html)? Triggering build commands was the first use case Watchman was designed for, so I expect it would work very well with peru. Filesystem watching tends to have a lot of complications and corner cases, and I'd be curious how watchdog and Watchman compare in cases like [rapid updates to many files](https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/cmd/trigger.html):

> Watchman waits for the filesystem to settle before processing any triggers, batching the list of changed files together before invoking the registered command. 

and [atomic-file-moving-instead-of-in-place-editing](https://github.com/gorakhargosh/watchdog):

> Vim does not modify files unless directed to do so. It creates backup files and then swaps them in to replace the files you are editing on the disk. This means that if you use Vim to edit your files, the on-modified events for those files will not be triggered by watchdog. You may need to configure Vim to appropriately to disable this feature.

I do not know why somebody is `stat`ing /etc/localtime or dylibs, but config_files are `stat`ed by powerline in order to determine whether they changed. To get rid of statting them you need to do one or both of the following:

- Install `pyuv`: this way it should stop statting config files and use macos fsevents daemon instead.
- Set `config.json/"common"/"interval"` to something non-zero (determines how often configuration changes are checked) or, better, `config.json/"common"/"reload_config"` to false (disables reloading configuration) by creating/editing ~/.config/powerline/config.json.

In both cases you will need to restart Vim and daemon. Restarting daemon is inevitable, in Vim you may use `:python import powerline; powerline.reload(); del powerline` instead (note: officially unstable functionality, but works fine most of time).

It took me a little while, but I got `zpython` building from source. That was not the fix, but upon continuing to dig, I now know my reasoning for the cause was incorrect.

I failed to realize that `zsh.getvalue()` returns an object of the type that matches the type of the `zsh` variable (I previously didn't know that shell variables had types other than string). Now, what seems to be the cause is that `_POWERLINE_JOBNUM`'s declaration in `powerline/bindings/zsh/powerline.zsh` does not include the global flag, so when `_powerline_set_jobnum()` is run later (I believe from a separate python process), `_POWERLINE_JOBNUM` is undefined, and `_POWERLINE_JOBNUM=${(%):-%j}` declares it as a string.

I was able to fix this by changing `integer _POWERLINE_JOBNUM` to `integer -g _POWERLINE_JOBNUM`. Perhaps I needed this because of how I am sourcing `powerline.zsh`; I am sourcing it in an `if` which is in a `for` loop which is in a function I defined in my `.zshrc`.

Is it inappropriate for `_POWERLINE_JOBNUM` to be declared as a global? Am I doing something wrong, or did I discover a weird edge case?

```python
database_factory = providers.Factory(

    SqlAlchemyDatabaseService,

The problem is that the Optimize API only allows to set the following options (this is the output of the `help()` function):

```
dump_benchmarks (bool) dump benchmarks for profiling (default: false)
elim_01 (bool) eliminate 01 variables (default: true)
enable_sat (bool) enable the new SAT core for propositional constraints (default: true)
enable_sls (bool) enable SLS tuning during weighted maxsast (default: false)
maxres.add_upper_bound_block (bool) restict upper bound with constraint (default: false)
maxres.hill_climb (bool) give preference for large weight cores (default: true)
maxres.max_core_size (unsigned int) break batch of generated cores if size reaches this number (default: 3)
maxres.max_correction_set_size (unsigned int) allow generating correction set constraints up to maximal size (default: 3)
maxres.max_num_cores (unsigned int) maximal number of cores per round (default: 4294967295)
maxres.maximize_assignment (bool) find an MSS/MCS to improve current assignment (default: false)
maxres.pivot_on_correction_set (bool) reduce soft constraints if the current correction set is smaller than current core (default: true)
maxres.wmax (bool) use weighted theory solver to constrain upper bounds (default: false)
maxsat_engine (symbol) select engine for maxsat: 'core_maxsat', 'wmax', 'maxres', 'pd-maxres' (default: maxres)
optsmt_engine (symbol) select optimization engine: 'basic', 'farkas', 'symba' (default: basic)
pb.compile_equality (bool) compile arithmetical equalities into pseudo-Boolean equality (instead of two inequalites) (default: false)
pp.neat (bool) use neat (as opposed to less readable, but faster) pretty printer when displaying context (default: true)
print_model (bool) display model for satisfiable constraints (default: false)
priority (symbol) select how to priortize objectives: 'lex' (lexicographic), 'pareto', or 'box' (default: lex)
rlimit (unsigned int) resource limit (0 means no limit) (default: 0)
timeout (unsigned int) timeout (in milliseconds) (UINT_MAX and 0 mean no timeout) (default: 4294967295)
```

Make sure your input layer is defined using the exact same size you're passing later. If your input layer contains a `1` somewhere, try replacing it by a `None` to make that dimension flexible -- Theano seems to have a bug in `theano.scan` somewhere that gives odd results when some input dimensions are fixed to `1`.

In Django 1.10 you cannot have "APP_DIRS" setting simultaneously with 'loaders' option set in "Templates".

The solution, in my case at least, was to remove 'APP_DIRS':True, but make sure include 'django.template.loaders.app_directories.Loader' in the 'loaders' option under 'Templates'.  These two settings are one in the same.
For more info on Djanog Template Loaders see [here](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/templates/api/#django.template.loaders.app_directories.Loader)

You can create `rs2::frame_queue` object and pass it as (instead of) the callback to start. 
Then you can do `rs2::frame f = q.wait_for_frame()` or `q.poll_for_frame(&f)`.
Frame queues should not be shared between multiple sensors. If you need to listen on multiple sensors, you can either create queue per sensor and poll on each one, or create an `rs2::syncer` object and pass it as a callback to multiple sensors. At this point, however, you will be essentially getting the same results as with the pipeline.

Netpyne data is separated into:
- netParams: network high-level specifications
- simConfig: simulation configuration
- net: network instance, including info on all pops, cells, conns, stims etc
- simData: simulation output data, including spkt times, voltage traces, etc

It is already possible to save and load each of these separately. For most of the graphs you will need the net and simData structures.

```python
import tensorflow_hub as hub
import tensorflow as tf
import numpy as np
tf.logging.set_verbosity(0)

def make_embed_fn(module):
  with tf.Graph().as_default():
    sentences = tf.placeholder(tf.string)
    embed = hub.Module(module)
    embeddings = embed(sentences)
    session = tf.train.MonitoredSession()
  return lambda x: session.run(embeddings, {sentences: x})

f2 = make_embed_fn("https://tfhub.dev/google/universal-sentence-encoder-large/2")

x = [
  "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog.",
  "I am a sentence for which I would like to get its embedding",
]

# Returns zeros showing the module is stable across instantiations.
print(np.linalg.norm(f2(x) - f2_dup(x)))

# ISSUE: returns 0.3 showing the embeddings per example depend on other elements in the batch.
print(np.linalg.norm(f2(x[0:1]) - f2(x)[0:1]))
```

Talking to @darconeous, here is our suggestion to address this:
- Will add a new spinel property called `THREAD_ALLOW_LOCAL_NET_DATA_CHANGE` which is `boolean`. 
- This property should be set to `true` before modifying any of local net data properties (e.g., inserting a new value to `ON_MESH_NETS` or a `LOCAL_ROUTES`, otherwise, we get an `SPINEL_STATUS_INVALID_STATE` error if we try to write to these properties).
- When this property transitions from `true` to `false`, we register all the net data changes with leader. 

This serves couple of purposes:
- It provide a way from wpantund to use  the flexibility of OT to update multiple net data properties before registering the changes with leader.
- It ensures only one entity is making the changes (this is not immediately applicable with current wpantund model but may be helpful in future).

I am working on changes to address this. 

Any thoughts/suggestions?

@nibanks >> Why are we treating the code that references the APIs any different than the implementation? Generally, they would both be built for the same platform, and have the same requirements/restrictions, correct?

The "wrap_stdint.h"changes are only in the internal implementation. It is private and internal to openthread, it is not a public thing, and will not be exposed in that way. What you describe is basically making this a public thing.

This is a requirement per @darconeous 

All other platforms are C99 compliant, and provide a file "stdint.h" - MS Visual Studio, specifically the kernel build (_K projects) are not C99 compliant because they do not use a newer visual studio.

Thus this is a windows only problem. Some how that build environment must provide a file called: "stdint.h" and it needs to be 100% private to the windows build.

So the question is: Where do you think this should be put?  

And you should, note - that if the windows platform changes to a newer compiler it will need to change the include path (deleting this file) or removing this file such that the compiler provided stdint.h file is used instead.

Thoughts?

Do we both agree that an owner is definitely needed for reference counting purposes if the ENSURECOPY flag is not specified to NumPy? (by owner I mean what is passed via the ``obj`` argument to e.g. PyArray_NewFromDescr) -- without an owner, there is no mechanism to ensure that the referenced memory region stays alive while it is being referenced. The issue of who is allowed to read/write is also important but orthogonal to this issue.

Now let's say that the user wants to copy the array contents. In that case I don't see why it would be useful to specify an owner since the lifetimes are completely decoupled.

So as far as I can see, a default argument is sufficient. There is no need to add a ``ensurecopy`` flag analogous to the current strict casting/memory layout flags.

BTW: I am pretty sure that NumPy will allocate a new memory region if the "slave" NumPy array gets a reshape request, since it does not own that memory region and thus can't just go and free it.

Regarding your code example: to be super-strict, I think it would have to be

```
/* owner = */ py::cast(self)
```

Chris, PTAL. One small change.

In the end, since the return code from the LZ4 library block decompression function doesn't differentiate between the output buffer size being too small, I've settled on an approach that allows you to write code like this:

```python
import lz4.block

input = b'0' * 255
c = lz4.block.compress(input, store_size=False)
usize = 20
while True:
    try:
        d = lz4.block.decompress(c, uncompressed_size=usize)
        break
    except lz4.block.LZ4BlockError:
        usize *= 2
assert d == input
```
So, the key changes are:
* `uncompressedd_size`, when greater than zero, is now not strictly checked, but is regarded as an upper bound on the output size.
* A new exception which is raised if the output buffer size is too small to hold the uncompressed data, or if the compressed data is somehow invalid.

This code can be seen on the `block_unknown_size` branch.

This would differ if you're handling mostly text data.  In that case serialization will kill you anyway.  Generally speaking my guess is that very few workloads of this type are at the point where zero-copy is something they should worry about.

@devin-petersohn So this automatically happens for objects that are backed by object store memory (this is true for numpy arrays and objects that contain numpy arrays). For example.

```python
x_id = ray.put(np.zeros(10))
x = ray.get(x)
```

As long as `x` is in scope, then the corresponding object in the object store will not be evicted.

As for changing the eviction policy, we would ultimately like to factor that out nicely as something that can be changed easily. It's implemented in https://github.com/apache/arrow/blob/master/cpp/src/plasma/eviction_policy.h.

To answer your question about loading from files, I'm not sure what the best implementation is, but we may want to evict to disk in a way that allows us to have random access without scanning. But all of this is somewhat hypothetical at the moment. We're interested in feedback on the best practice here.

Choosing between evicting/persisting based on cost of recreation is something that in principle should be doable and we don't want to rule it out, but it also sounds pretty complicated, so for now I'd prefer to stick with simple heuristics.

`LocalizedCharField` and `LocalizedTextField` following the [Django convention](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/models/fields/#null) for string-based fields uses the empty string as value for “no data”, not NULL as LocalizedField. It is best to demonstrate by example:

```
# Suppose we have a model

class MyModel(models.Model):
    localized_field = LocalizedField(null=True)
    char_field = LocalizedCharField(null=True)     # in fact for this field type null=True is not necessary

# Then we create new instance and save this

>>> instance = MyModel()
>>> instance.save()

# In result, we have

>>> instance.char_field
LocalizedStingValue<{'ro': '', 'nl': '', 'en': ''}> 0x140074115948360

>>> instance.localized_field
LocalizedValue<{'ro': None, 'nl': None, 'en': None}> 0x140074115947800

# in template we will get "None" for LocalizedField and empty string for LocalizedCharField

>>> str(instance.char_field.en)
'None'

>>> str(instance.localized_field.en)
''

```
The end result in the database is different: `localized_field = null` and `char_field = 'en => , ro => , nl => ' `(or if value will be present for one lang we will get for `localized_field = 'en => 'test', ro => null, nl => null'`, and `char_field = 'en => test, ro => , nl => '`).

If `LocalizedField` will handle "no data" in django way, then `LocalizedStringValue` and `LocalizedTextField` is not needed, this was added as not violating backward compatibility of `LocalizedField`.

Next, yes, currently ``LocalizedCharField`` and ``LocalizedTextField`` has only one difference is the associated form field. And yes, that can be handled in a different way (define widget in form class for example), but as a end-user of ``django-localized-fields`` can say that it would be very nice to have field with ``<input>`` widget out of box and do not redefine widget for each field in each form or/and not copy paste custom field implementation from project to project.

I can add `max_length` parametter and validator as in django `CharField`, for more functionality.

Finally, I absolutely agree about the tests and will add these.

The root cause is mainly changes to RequestValidator to support correct `grant_type` as describe in #484 and use `Authorization: Bearer` to send tokens as described in #488:

Example old way to send a token request:

```
curl -X POST \
    -H "Cache-Control: no-cache" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
    "http://127.0.0.1:8000/o/token/" \
    -d "client_id=qLSq5KfSvYRho..." \
    -d "client_secret=bhOWtOxm1..." \
    -d "code=B8J3PQBpDeHFohgrVnvs26KrTAMNhI" \
    -d "redirect_uri=http://localhost/callback" \
    -d "grant_type=openid"
```
Note the wrong use of `grant_type=openid`

Example fixed way to send a token request:

```
curl -X POST \
    -H "Cache-Control: no-cache" \
    -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
    "http://127.0.0.1:8000/o/token/" \
    -d "client_id=qLSq5KfSvYRho..." \
    -d "client_secret=bhOWtOxm1..." \
    -d "code=B8J3PQBpDeHFohgrVnvs26KrTAMNhI" \
    -d "redirect_uri=http://localhost/callback" \
    -d "grant_type=authorization_code"
```

Now note the correct use `grant_type=authorization_code`

No more need to use `Authorization: JWT eyJhbGciOiAiUlMyNTYifQ...` use as:

```
curl -vv --header "Content-Type: application/json" \
         --header "Accept: application/json; indent=4" \
         --header "Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiAiUlMyNTYifQ..." \
         http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1/users/
```

Note the header `Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiAiUlMyNTYifQ...`

I can see a way to make those changes backward compatible, fell free provide a PR if you found a way.
